chemicals.chemical_formula: The chemical formula for amygdalin is C
chemicals.name: Amygdalin (and its toxic metabolite, Hydrogen Cyanide)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Plum pits (kernels) naturally contain very high concentrations of amygdalin, ranging from 4 to 17.5 g/kg depending on the specific plum variety9. This gives plum kernels an exceptionally high cyanogenic potential, capable of yielding roughly 696 mg of hydrogen cyanide per kilogram of seed material10. The hydrogen cyanide released from this compound is extremely potent; the fatal oral d
chemicals.description: in cats: Amygdalin itself is not directly toxic; rather, it acts as a precursor to a deadly poison14. When a cat chews or crushes a plum pit (seed), the physical damage to the plant tissue allows amygdalin to mix with β-glucosidase enzymes, which are also present in the cat's intestinal tract4.... These enzymes hydrolyze the amygdalin, ultimately decomposing it to rapidly release hydrogen cyanide
chemicals.name: The formula for hydrogen cyanide is HCN

description: Plum flesh (Prunus domestica) is not toxic to cats in small amounts, but the pit, stems, and leaves contain amygdalin — a cyanogenic glycoside that releases hydrogen cyanide (HCN) when chewed. This is the same mechanism as apricot and peach pit toxicity. The flesh alone, offered without the pit, is safe as an occasional tiny treat.
emergencyNote: The toxic components of plums are found in the pits (seeds), stems, and leaves, which contain cyanogenic glycosides
name: Plums
safetyNotes: Pit, stems, and leaves: toxic — amygdalin yields HCN via beta-glucosidase cleavage
Mechanism: HCN inhibits cytochrome c oxidase, halting cellular respiration
Symptoms from pit ingestion: rapid breathing, weakness, drooling, vomiting; severe cases cause seizures and respiratory failure
Ripe flesh: safe in very small amounts, but offers no nutritional benefit and is high in sugar
Always remove the pit completely before offering any fruit; avoid dried plums (prunes) as high fibre causes diarrhea

symptoms.name: The symptoms a cat shows after ingesting or being exposed to plums, particularly the toxic pits
Difficulty Breathing and Hyperventilation
symptoms.notes: If a cat chews or crushes a plum pit, it releases hydrogen cyanide, which inhibits the cells' ability to utilize oxygen12. This systemic suffocation forces the cat to hyperventilate as the body struggles to compensate
symptoms.onset: Rapid onset (often within minutes to a few hours).

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Abdominal Pain
symptoms.notes: These gastrointestinal signs can occur as an initial reaction to cyanide toxicity or from the physical irritation of the plant material and fruit passing through the digestive tract
symptoms.onset: Shortly after ingestion (often within a few hours).

symptoms.name: Trouble Walking and Mental Confusion
symptoms.notes: The sudden lack of cellular energy (ATP) production caused by cyanide quickly impairs the central nervous system, leading to profound dizziness, nerve damage, and an inability to walk properly

symptoms.name: Systemic Shock and Coma
symptoms.notes: Because the brain and heart have the highest oxygen demands in the body, the cyanide-induced halt in cellular respiration rapidly starves these vital organs of power, resulting in abnormally low blood pressure, shock, loss of consciousness, and death if left untreated

symptoms.name: Cherry-Red Skin or Mucous Membranes
symptoms.notes: Because cyanide blocks the cells from extracting oxygen from the bloodstream, the venous blood remains fully oxygenated4. This causes the cat's skin, gums, and mucous membranes to turn an abnormally bright, cherry-red color

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal or Throat Obstruction
symptoms.notes: Aside from the chemical dangers of a crushed seed, swallowing an intact, hard plum pit poses a major mechanical hazard26. The seed can easily become lodged in the cat's throat or block the intestinal tract, requiring emergency surgical removal
symptoms.onset: Immediate (if causing choking) or variable (can take hours to days if lodged further down in the int

treatments.name: The toxic components of plums are found in the pits (seeds), stems, and leaves, which contain cyanogenic glycosides

treatments.description: of the procedure: Placing the cat in an oxygen cage or providing flow-by oxygen to maximize the amount of oxygen available in the bloodstream3. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Supplemental Oxygen Therapy (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is an absolute critical priority for a symptomatic cat. The cyanide released by crushed plum seeds is a potent inhibitor of cytochrome c oxidase, which completely halts cellular ATP (energy) production and causes rapid cell death1. Because the toxin prevents cells from utilizing oxygen, aggress

treatments.description: of the procedure: A veterinarian administers an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist, such as dexmedetomidine, to safely force the cat to vomit and expel the plum pit or plant material from the stomach before more cyanide can be released and absorbed4. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This intervention is the primary goal if the ingestion is caught very early (within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is still asymptomatic4. Pet owners must never attempt to use hydrogen peroxide to induce vomiting at home, as it is strictly contraindicated for cats and can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastriti

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and gastrointestinal tract4. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered immediately following successful emesis to trap any remaining cyanogenic compounds in the gut and prevent them from entering the bloodstream

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to maintain perfusion, hydration, and support failing organ systems3. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.notes: Because there is no specific antidote available for cyanide toxicity in felines, aggressive IV fluid therapy and intensive supportive care are essential to sustain the cat's cardiovascular system through the resulting metabolic shock

treatments.description: of the procedure: Utilizing an endoscope or performing abdominal surgery to physically extract an intact plum pit from the digestive tract. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Mechanical Extraction (Obstruction Management)
treatments.notes: While crushed plum pits release deadly cyanide, swallowing a hard, intact pit presents a different but equally dangerous physical hazard. Fruit seeds are hard and difficult to digest, and swallowing them can easily cause a dangerous mechanical obstruction in the cat's esophagus, stomach, or intestin
